title: ResponseAI — Proposal for Customer Experience
snippet: ScribeFlow's drafting assistant proposal claiming 70% acceptance rate, $1.5M annual capacity creation, 10-week implementation. Includes Forrester/McKinsey/Gartner benchmark references.

title: Customer Support Operations — Current Architecture (Q1 2026)
snippet: 50 agents across Americas / EMEA / APAC pods, Salesforce + Zendesk hybrid, 18 months tagged email history (~480k tickets), no ML team in CX, executive sponsor Priya Sharma (SVP CX).

title: CX-421 — Evaluate AI response drafting for support agent productivity
snippet: Open Jira ticket (Reporter: Daniel Ortiz VP CX, Assignee: Priya Sharma) for FY26 cost-to-serve initiative. Comments include vendor-claim skepticism (Priya) and PII/data-residency flag (Aisha Khan, Privacy).

title: AI Fraud Detection Pilot — Post-Mortem Lessons Learned (2024)
snippet: Retro on the 2024 fraud-detection ML rollout. Three lessons: dedicated MLOps owner is non-negotiable; vendor 'acceptance' metrics ≠ production reality; rejection-feedback labels must be captured from day one.
content:
## Background

In 2024 Deluxe Risk deployed a vendor ML fraud-detection system across consumer transactions. The model performed well in vendor benchmarks but underdelivered in production through year 1.

## What we learned

1. **Dedicated MLOps owner is non-negotiable.** Risk didn't have an embedded data scientist. The vendor's 'managed tuning' service turned out to be ad-hoc quarterly retrains. By month 6 the model's false-positive rate had drifted 40% from pilot baseline. No one inside Deluxe was watching.
2. **Vendor acceptance metrics are not production reality.** Vendor claimed 92% precision in pilot. Steady-state in production was 71%. The gap was real — vendor pilot was on a curated sample with clean labels; production sample is messier.
3. **Rejection-feedback labels MUST be captured from day one.** When the team finally tried to retrain in month 8, the rejection data wasn't labeled with 'why' it was rejected. The model couldn't learn from it.

## Recommendations for future AI projects

- Embed a data scientist or MLOps engineer into the team owning the model, before launch
- Negotiate a managed-tuning SLA with explicit performance targets and fee adjustments
- Instrument rejection/correction feedback collection into the workflow from day one
- Time-box pilots with a kill criterion before scaling

title: FY26 Cost-to-Serve Roadmap — Consumer Operations
snippet: Board-approved roadmap to cut consumer-ops cost-per-ticket by 20% in FY26. CX is the largest cost line; named levers: AI tooling, agent productivity, offshore arbitrage, channel deflection.
content:
## FY26 Cost-to-Serve Initiative

**Goal:** 20% reduction in cost-per-ticket across consumer operations by end of FY26.

**Board context:** Approved in Q4 FY25 board review. Tied to broader margin-expansion target. Daniel Ortiz (VP CX) and the Consumer Ops leadership team are accountable.

**Named levers (in priority order):**
1. AI tooling (drafting, deflection, knowledge surfacing) — primary lever, $3-5M target
2. Agent productivity programs (training, ramp acceleration) — secondary, $1-2M target
3. Offshore arbitrage on tier-1 support — secondary, $1-3M target (politically sensitive)
4. Channel deflection (self-serve, chatbot expansion) — tertiary, $0.5-1M target

**Reporting:** Monthly to CFO, quarterly to board.

**Risks already flagged:**
- AI tooling depends on vendor delivery + internal MLOps capacity (gap noted)
- Offshore arbitrage carries reputational risk if scaled too aggressively
- Self-serve has historically capped at ~25% deflection

title: Salesforce Service Cloud Integration Patterns — Engineering Reference
snippet: Engineering reference for Salesforce Service Cloud integrations. Covers API rate limits, custom object schema for cases, AppExchange-vs-custom decision tree, and the standard side-panel widget pattern.
content:
## Salesforce Service Cloud Integration Patterns

### Standard patterns we use

**Side-panel widget**: For tools that surface data alongside a case (e.g., draft suggestions, knowledge articles), embed via Lightning Component in the case sidebar. ~2-week implementation for new vendors.

**Custom case object fields**: For vendor-injected metadata (e.g., AI confidence scores, draft acceptance), use namespaced custom fields. Avoid polluting Salesforce-standard fields.

**API rate limits**: Salesforce Service Cloud caps at 100k API calls / 24h for our edition. Real-time integrations should batch / cache.

**Single sign-on**: Use SAML via Azure AD for vendor login (same auth as the rest of CX tooling).

title: EMEA Pod Expansion Business Case (2024)
snippet: 2024 business case to expand the EMEA support pod from 12 to 16 agents. Approved but under-delivered due to hiring delays. Includes EMEA-specific tone and language considerations relevant to any AI rollout.
content:
## EMEA Pod Expansion (FY24)

**Approved:** Q1 2024. **Delivered:** Q4 2024 (3 quarters late).

**Backstory:** EMEA pod was 12 agents handling 30% of inbound volume but 45% of complex tier-2/3 escalations. Ramp time for new EMEA hires was 9-10 weeks vs 6-8 in Americas due to language fluency screening.

**Relevance to AI tooling:** Any AI drafting solution must accommodate EMEA-specific tone (more formal, less American conversational) and at least passive multilingual support (English/French/German). Vendor pilots done on US English data will need EMEA-specific calibration.

title: CSAT Quarterly Review — Q4 2025
snippet: Q4 2025 customer satisfaction review. CSAT averaged 4.1/5 across pods. Notable: first-response time inversely correlated with CSAT (each hour of delay = 0.1 CSAT point drop on tickets exceeding 4h SLA).
content:
## Q4 2025 CSAT Review

Overall CSAT: 4.1/5 (target 4.2). EMEA pod at 3.9 dragged blended down.

**Key finding:** First-response time is the strongest CSAT lever we have. Each additional hour beyond the 4h SLA drops average CSAT for that ticket by ~0.1 point.

**Implication for AI tooling:** Anything that reduces first-response time (faster drafting, faster triage) has CSAT upside beyond pure capacity savings. Estimated value: $200k-400k/yr in churn-prevention terms, though we haven't been able to isolate causation cleanly.

title: Generative AI Procurement Guide — Vendor Evaluation Criteria
snippet: Procurement standard for evaluating GenAI vendors. Covers PII handling, data residency, model-IP ownership, indemnification, and tuning-service SLAs. Required reading before any GenAI vendor contract.
content:
## GenAI Vendor Evaluation Criteria

**Required clauses for any GenAI vendor contract:**

1. **PII handling**: Vendor must NOT use customer data for model training outside our tenant. Get this in writing in the MSA.
2. **Data residency**: For EMEA customers, data must stay in EU. Vendor must confirm region pinning.
3. **Model-IP ownership**: Fine-tuned weights belong to us. Vendor cannot use them for other customers.
4. **Indemnification**: Vendor must indemnify for IP-infringement claims on model outputs.
5. **Tuning-service SLA**: If vendor markets a 'managed' product, performance targets and remedy must be in the contract. 'Best efforts' is not enough.
6. **Exit clause**: Ability to export training data and switch vendors with reasonable notice.

title: Forrester Wave: AI in Customer Service, Q1 2025
snippet: External benchmark. Leaders include ScribeFlow, Forethought, ASAPP, Yellow.ai. Blended draft-acceptance across enterprise deployments 35-55%; leaders hit 60-65% after 4-6 months of tuning. Caveat: vendor 'pilot' acceptance rates routinely 15-25 percentage points above steady-state.
content:
## Forrester Wave: AI in Customer Service — Q1 2025 (summary)

**Leaders:** ScribeFlow, Forethought, ASAPP, Yellow.ai.

**Strong Performers:** Cresta, Ada, Drift.

**Key data points:**
- Blended draft-acceptance across enterprise deployments: **35-55%** (median 42%)
- Leaders reach **60-65%** after 4-6 months of internal tuning
- **Vendor pilot acceptance rates run 15-25 percentage points above steady-state** across the entire category — this is a structural pattern, not vendor-specific
- Ticket complexity is the single largest predictor of acceptance ceiling
- Deployments without dedicated MLOps function plateau within 6 months

**Forrester's recommendation:** Plan against the lower end of vendor projections. Treat the upper end as a stretch case, not a baseline.
